planting.note: Plant small tuber clusters in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; rue anemone is a spring ephemeral that dies back completely by early summer — mark its location to avoid disturbing it during summer dormancy.

directions: Plant small tuber clusters in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial to full shade — rue anemone is one of the smallest and most delicate of all eastern woodland spring ephemerals, producing clusters of white to pale pink (rarely deep pink) five-petaled flowers on slender stems only 4–9 inches tall in early spring, just as the forest canopy begins to leaf out. The leaves closely resemble those of meadow rue (Thalictrum), explaining both the common name and the former genus placement. Like all true spring ephemerals it dies back completely by early summer, leaving no trace above ground. It is a slow-growing but extremely long-lived native that gradually forms a charming colony when well-sited. Named varieties like 'Shoaf's Double Pink' and 'Betty Blake' (double white flowers) are available from specialty native plant nurseries. An outstanding native for the shaded wildflower garden, best combined with later-emerging foliage plants to fill in after it goes dormant.

planting.note: Direct sow seed in fall or start by division in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich to average, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; blue-stemmed goldenrod is one of the very few goldenrods that blooms reliably in shade, making it irreplaceable for the shaded fall garden.

directions: Grow in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich to average, well-drained soil in partial to full shade — blue-stemmed goldenrod is one of the most valuable native goldenrods for woodland and shaded gardens, distinguished by its distinctive blue-green to purple-blue stems and its genuine shade tolerance in a genus that is almost entirely full-sun. The small golden flower clusters emerge from the leaf axils along the upper portion of the arching stems in September and October, creating a wreath-like effect (explaining the alternate common name 'wreath goldenrod'). It grows naturally in moist, rich woodland understories throughout the eastern US and is well-adapted to the dry shade under large trees where most goldenrods cannot survive. An outstanding plant for bringing late-season native pollinator value into the shaded garden when almost no other native plants are blooming. The seeds provide fall food for native sparrows and other seed-eating birds.

planting.note: Direct sow seed in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to average, sandy to rocky soil in full sun to partial shade; wild petunia self-sows prolifically in appropriate conditions and can naturalize in dry, open areas — a welcome attribute in a native meadow but worth noting in a more formal setting.

directions: Sow seed in fall in well-drained, dry to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade — wild petunia (Ruellia humilis) is a native prairie perennial that produces a continuous succession of lavender to pale violet, 1.5-inch funnel-shaped flowers remarkably similar in appearance to garden petunias through summer and into fall, making it a genuinely ornamental native for dry, sunny or partly shaded settings. Individual flowers last only a day, but new ones open continuously over many weeks on the low, hairy, sessile-leaved plants. It grows naturally in dry, open prairies, rocky glades, and open woodland edges. Unlike tropical Ruellia (Mexican petunia, R. simplex), native wild petunia is not invasive — it self-sows modestly and is well-behaved in most gardens. Excellent for rock gardens, dry slopes, and native plant gardens. An important nectar source for bumblebees and long-tongued native bees.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Canada anemone spreads aggressively by rhizome and seed and is best used where mass coverage is desired or where natural spreading is welcome — it will crowd out less competitive plants.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade — Canada anemone is one of the most vigorous native groundcovers available, producing a long-lasting display of pure white, 1.5-inch five-petaled flowers above attractive, deeply lobed leaves from late spring through early summer, then spreading steadily by rhizome to form a dense, weed-suppressing colony. It grows naturally in moist prairies, stream banks, and moist, open woodland edges from Canada south to the mid-Atlantic states. Its aggressive spreading habit makes it ideal for naturalizing large, moist areas along pond edges, woodland margins, and rain garden overflows — it quickly colonizes disturbed, moist ground and crowd out invasive weeds. Use it with appropriate caution in smaller gardens where it may overwhelm neighbors. An outstanding erosion-control plant for moist, sunny to partly shaded slopes. Important early-season nectar source.

planting.note: Direct sow seed in fall in well-drained to average, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to moist soil in partial shade; tall thimbleweed self-sows readily and naturalizes in appropriate dry to moist woodland conditions without being invasive.

directions: Sow seed in fall in well-drained to average, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to moist soil in partial shade — tall thimbleweed is an upright, woodland-edge native anemone whose white, 1–1.5 inch flowers are followed by the plant's most distinctive feature: elongated, thimble-shaped seed heads that are covered in cottony white fibers (resembling a thimble wrapped in white fluff) and persist well into winter, providing long-lasting ornamental value. The flowers attract solitary native bees and small beetles. It grows naturally in dry to moist open woodland, woodland edges, and clearings throughout the eastern and central US, tolerating the dry shade beneath dense tree canopies better than most native wildflowers. The persistent cottony seed heads are useful in dried arrangements. More drought-tolerant than most anemone species. An excellent and underused native for dry, shaded woodland garden settings.
